moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with the Réti Opening's characteristic quiet move—the English Opening. This move controls d5 and e5 from the side rather than occupying the center directly, which is the hallmark of hypermodern play. By playing c4, we prepare to build a flexible pawn structure and often fianchetto our kingside bishop, putting long-term pressure on Black's center rather than rushing to occupy it.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: c4 controls d5 and e5 from the side, preparing a fianchetto to pressure Black's center long-term.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black immediately supports their d5 advance with c6, preparing a classic Slav setup. This move is solid and prevents us from easily controlling d5 with a future d4, while also preparing ...dxc4 if we ever push c5. The move tells us Black wants a principled, positional battle rather than a sharp tactical one.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: c6 supports d5, prevents easy d4 control, and preps ...dxc4 — Black wants a positional battle.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We develop our knight to its ideal square, controlling the important d4 and e5 squares while staying flexible about our pawn structure. The knight on f3 supports a potential kingside fianchetto and prepares us to play d4 later if we want a more central presence. This move is the foundation of the Anglo-Slav, where we develop methodically before committing our pawns.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Nf3 controls d4 and e5, supports a kingside fianchetto, and keeps pawn structure flexible in the Anglo-Slav.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black stakes their claim to the center, which is the natural follow-up to ...c6. Now Black has central control and we must decide whether to exchange on d5, challenge with cxd5, or let the tension remain. This pawn on d5 will become a key feature of the middlegame, and Black is fighting for active piece play.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's d5 pawn, from the Caro-Kann, now controls the center — White must decide whether to exchange, challenge, or maintain tension.

moveAnnotations.annotation: This move reveals our fianchetto setup—we're building the long diagonal to put pressure on the entire board from a2 to g8. Playing g3 is flexible and solid; we don't commit to d4 yet, instead letting Black show their hand. The g3 move exemplifies the Réti philosophy of controlling space and weak squares (like d5) without occupying the center with pawns.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: g3 fianchettoes the bishop, pressuring the a2–g8 diagonal and controlling d5 without committing to d4.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops their kingside knight naturally, supporting the center and preparing to potentially challenge our fianchettoed bishop with ...Ne4 or ...Nh5 later. The knight on f6 is well-placed for both defense and counterplay. This is consistent with Black's solid Slav approach.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's knight on f6 supports the center and eyes Ne4 or Nh5 to challenge the fianchettoed bishop.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We complete our fianchetto setup, placing the bishop on the long diagonal where it exerts pressure on d5 and controls important central squares. The bishop on g2 is a defining feature of the Anglo-Slav; it subtly pressures Black's center and discourages moves like ...dxc4 or ...e5 because of the bishop's influence. From here, we have a flexible position where we can still play d4, or maintain flexibility with moves like cxd5 or Qb3.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: The fianchettoed bishop on g2 pressures d5, discouraging ...dxc4 or ...e5 while keeping d4, cxd5, or Qb3 flexible.
